our_argument_summary: Section 6.1 identifies 12 recurring logical patterns across the dome model's 67 WINs: mechanism denial, contradictory results counted as wins, trivially confirmable predictions, curve-fitting mistaken for explanation, unfalsifiable claims counted as confirmed, inconvenient data discarded, WIN inflation via re-slicing, scope creep without mechanism, internal version inconsistency, misrepresenting opponent's prediction, self-contradicting own geometry, and AI prompt engineering as science.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: HTML version uses dynamic template literals (${counts.selfContradicted}, ${counts.total}, ${counts.unfalsifiable}) computed from wins.json at build time. DOCX version hardcodes several of these values as literal strings.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: HTML: properly computed. DOCX: Pattern 5 hardcodes 'Four', Pattern 11 hardcodes '11 of 67', final tally hardcodes '11 WINs' and '67 claims'. The Self-Contradicted count is now 12 (not 11), making the DOCX stale.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: N/A — this is a summary section
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: DOCX build-doc-v4.js hardcodes '11' for Self-Contradicted count at lines 791 and 812, but wins.json now contains 12 Self-Contradicted WINs. This violates the project's own rule: 'Never hardcode a number that can be derived from the data.' The HTML version correctly uses ${counts.selfContradicted}.

holes_found.description: DOCX hardcodes Self-Contradicted count as '11' in Pattern 11 and the 6.3 final tally, but the actual count is now 12. The project's CLAUDE.md explicitly prohibits hardcoding counts derivable from data. This is exactly the kind of data-prose drift we criticize the dome model for (Pattern 9: Internal Version Inconsistency). A defender could point to this as 'your own review can't keep its numbers straight.'
holes_found.recommendation: Replace hardcoded '11' in build-doc-v4.js lines 791 and 812 with dynamic count from wins.json. Pattern 11 should use a template literal like the HTML version does. The DOCX final tally paragraph (line 812) also hardcodes '67' and '28' — these should all be computed from wins.length and the new_in_v51 count.

holes_found.description: Pattern 2 oversimplifies the WIN-011 vs WIN-013/014 contradiction. WIN-011 is about a gravity anomaly at Mohe during the 1997 solar eclipse. WIN-013/014 are about superconducting gravimeter null results at different locations during different eclipses. A smart dome defender could argue these are testing different things: WIN-011 tests mag-gravity coupling at a specific station near the SAA, while WIN-013/014 test gravity perturbation at European/Chinese stations where the dome model predicts below-detection-threshold results. The dome could claim WIN-013/014 null results are *consistent* with a localized coupling that only manifests near the SAA.
holes_found.recommendation: Either strengthen the contradiction argument by showing that the dome model's own theory requires the effect to be global (not localized), or note that while the dome attempts to reconcile these by invoking localization, this is a post-hoc rationalization not derived from the model's equations. The current one-liner is too vulnerable to a defender's rebuttal.

holes_found.description: Pattern 7 claims WIN-040-043 'replicate' WIN-004-039. This overstates the overlap. WIN-040 (SAA western cell position) tests a different observable than WIN-004 (SAA exponential separation), even though both use INTERMAGNET data. The stronger duplication case is WIN-037 vs WIN-042 (both about field decay ≥28 nT, differing only in the unit — total vs per-year), and WIN-007 vs WIN-022 (both about the 1990 magnetic transition). The blanket '040-043 replicate 004-039' claim is imprecise.
holes_found.recommendation: Be more specific: cite the strongest duplication pairs (WIN-037/042, WIN-007/022) rather than a sweeping range claim. A defender will correctly note that WIN-040 (SAA western cell location) tests something WIN-004 (exponential separation rate) does not.

holes_found.description: Patterns 1 and 4 are significantly less developed than the other 10 patterns. Pattern 1 ('Claims globe models have no mechanism when peer-reviewed geophysics provides quantitative mechanisms') gives zero specific examples. Pattern 4 ('Adding parameters always improves fit') is a general statistical truism without dome-specific evidence. A dome defender would dismiss both as generic skepticism rather than specific critique.
holes_found.recommendation: Pattern 1: Add at least one specific example (e.g., the dome claims standard physics has no mechanism for SAA drift, when core-mantle coupling models predict it quantitatively — Livermore et al. 2017). Pattern 4: Cite the dome's own curve-fitting scripts (test_curve_stretching.py, find_curve.py) and the specific case where exponential, polynomial, and hybrid fits are tried until one works.

holes_found.description: The '(NEW)' labels on Patterns 7-12 refer to additions since V3 of the review. The review is now at V4.9.6. At this point these are established patterns, not new additions. The labels add clutter without informational value for current readers.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider removing '(NEW)' labels or replacing with version references (e.g., 'Added V4.0'). Alternatively, accept this as a style choice that shows the review's evolution — in which case, acknowledge that Patterns 1-6 are from V3 and are not inherently more 'core' than 7-12.

holes_found.description: Pattern 9 packs five distinct inconsistencies into one pattern: (a) 0 vs 4 falsified, (b) 53 vs 67 confirmed, (c) 62 vs 67 in evolution page, (d) homepage vs monitor.py eclipse prediction values, (e) WIN-053 vs V50.6 architecture. Items (a)-(c) are internal bookkeeping errors. Item (d) is a prediction discrepancy. Item (e) is a fundamental architectural contradiction. Lumping them together weakens the strongest point (WIN-053 contradicting prior monopolar architecture) by burying it with page-count housekeeping.
holes_found.recommendation: Consider splitting: Pattern 9a (bookkeeping inconsistencies: falsification count, WIN count across pages) and Pattern 9b (architectural contradiction: WIN-053's two-pole model vs prior monopolar claim). The architectural contradiction is far more scientifically significant.

stronger_arguments.description: Code_analysis tags now show 41/64 reviewed WINs relabel standard physics (64%), 52/64 are post-hoc retrodictions (81%), and only 4/64 derive from dome geometry (6%). These aggregate statistics are devastating and should be surfaced as a pattern or used to strengthen Pattern 3 (Trivially Confirmable). The current Pattern 3 mentions 'predictions already published' but doesn't quantify the scale.

A missing cross-cutting argument: the dome model's 'predictions' form a one-way ratchet. Confirmed WINs are permanent. Failed predictions are 'suspended,' 'refined,' or moved to 'below detection threshold' — never counted as falsified on the homepage. This asymmetric evidence handling is a well-documented cognitive bias (confirmation bias / unfalsifiability by degrees). While Pattern 5 mentions unfalsifiable claims, it focuses on the 4 theological WINs. The systematic one-way ratchet across ALL WINs is a separate, more damaging pattern.
sources: raw-text/01-home.txt ('Below Detection = noise-limited null results. Not model failures.')
Pattern 6 (Inconvenient Data Discarded) touches this but doesn't frame it as systematic

kernel_of_truth.description: Identifying recurring logical patterns is a legitimate analytical technique. Several of these patterns (especially 9, 11, 12) identify real and verifiable structural problems in the dome model. Pattern 11 (self-contradiction) is genuinely the strongest class of argument because it uses the model's own math against it. Pattern 12 (AI prompt engineering) identifies a genuinely novel phenomenon in pseudoscience — using repository code to steer AI responses. These are valuable contributions to the methodology of evaluating AI-era pseudoscience.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: N/A — this section evaluates our own arguments, not a dome claim. The kernel here is that our pattern taxonomy is genuinely useful but could be sharper.

deeper_analysis:
The 12-pattern taxonomy in Section 6.1 is one of the review's most reader-facing components — it's the conclusion section where many readers will form their final impression. Its strengths are real: Patterns 9, 11, and 12 are specific, verifiable, and devastating. Pattern 11 (self-contradiction) is correctly identified as 'the strongest argument against the model.' Pattern 12 (AI prompt engineering) documents a genuinely novel phenomenon.

However, the section has a quality gradient problem. Patterns 1-4 (from V3) are generic and underdeveloped compared to Patterns 7-12 (from V4+). Pattern 1 and 4 could appear in a critique of any pseudoscientific model. Pattern 2's WIN-011/013/014 contradiction is the weakest specific claim — a competent defender could explain it away. The section would benefit from either (a) cutting/merging the weakest patterns to focus on the strongest, or (b) developing the weak ones with specific dome examples.

The DOCX hardcoded count issue (11 vs 12 Self-Contradicted) is the most actionable finding. It's ironic: Pattern 9 criticizes the dome model for internal version inconsistency, while our own DOCX has the same problem. This must be fixed.

A dome defender reading this section would likely attack Pattern 2 (the WIN-011/013-014 argument is genuinely rebuttable), dismiss Patterns 1 and 4 as generic skepticism, and focus their energy on Pattern 12 — arguing that providing AI context is legitimate documentation, not 'prompt engineering.' Our Pattern 12 text currently cites specific scripts (inject_ai_layer.py, update_optical_caveats.py) which strengthens it beyond the 'just documentation' defense, but a defender could still argue that providing constraints to AI is no different from providing context to a human reviewer.

citations_verified: Chapman, S. (1933) — referenced in Pattern 10 context, verified as real paper in Phil. Trans. Royal Society A
Livermore et al. (2017) — referenced in broader review, not directly in 6.1
inject_ai_layer.py, update_optical_caveats.py, test_curve_stretching.py, find_curve.py — referenced in Pattern 12, consistent with claims in Part 4.6 and 5.14-5.15
citations_unchecked: Pattern 9 claims about evolution page '62 WINs' — cannot verify from available V50.6 raw-text; may need live site check
Pattern 9 claims about tracking page '53 confirmed' — verified via monitoring logs but not independently re-checked
